title: Architecture Decision Record: Graduated Blowout Backup Rotation
scope: Replace the single-threshold blowout trigger with a graduated three-tier system that begins earlier, escalates with the lead, and pairs substitution decisions with conservative play-calling.

rationale.item: Starters played full games in 20-24 point leads. A team up 22 in the 3rd quarter would run its full starting lineup through the end of the game. This inflated individual stat lines, padded scoring totals, and looked unrealistic.

Backups accumulated near-zero stats. Without meaningful game reps, backup players had no opportunity to build stat profiles. In dynasty mode, this matters: a backup Halfback who never touches the ball has no case for portal interest or development.

Blowout final scores were too high. With starters running aggressive play-calls (kick passes, laterals, trick plays) deep into decided games, final scores ballooned. The engine had no mechanism to shift to conservative, clock-chewing football when the game was over in all but name.

Why graduated tiers instead of a lower flat threshold? A flat threshold would be too aggressive in many situations. An 18-point lead early in Q3 can evaporate — pulling all starters at that point risks giving the game away. Graduated tiers let coaches respond proportionally.

Why couple play-calling changes with substitutions? Substitutions alone don't solve the scoring problem. Even with backups in the game, if the play-caller is still dialing up kick passes and trick plays, the offense generates unnecessary scoring opportunities and turnover risk.

actions.description: Implemented _blowout_tier(score_diff) returning tiers 0-3 (Normal / Soft Rotation / Pull Starters / Deep Bench) based on lead size, quarter, and time remaining, including late-Q2 triggers and a Q4 under-5-minute rule at tier 2.

Soft rotation logic (tier 1): filter to skill-position starters (Viper, Zeroback, Halfback, Wingback, Slotback), sort by game_touches descending, 60% chance each of being rested for one drive (cap 2 per evaluation); additive to performance and fatigue checks.

Conservative play-calling (blowout clock-chew) in select_play_family(): run families 2.0x, sweep_option 1.4x, speed_option 1.2x, kick_pass 0.20x, lateral_spread 0.10x, trick_play 0.05x, viper_jet 0.30x, snap_kick/field_goal 1.3x. Q4 clock-run mode now applies universally when leading in Q4.
Tempo play-clock milking: leading team in a blowout runs base_time floored at 36 seconds and capped at 42, stacking with existing 3-minute-warning clock burn logic.

Expanded _is_blowout(): from '20+ pts, Q3/Q4 only' to '20+ pts Q3/Q4, or 25+ pts Q2 with ≤5:00 left', so backup-friendly touch distribution (_spread_the_love_offense/_spread_the_love_defense) also kicks in earlier.

risk_posture: Tier 1 rotation is probabilistic. ... This is intentional (coaching variance) but could look inconsistent in small samples.
Clock-chew stacks aggressively. ... This is correct behavior for a blowout, but worth monitoring for games where the lead narrows and the play-caller should open back up.
The thresholds are 5 points higher than Q3+ equivalents to account for the half remaining.

validation_claims.claim: 20-game mismatch test (Alabama vs Alabama State, seeds 100-119): avg home score 102.5, avg away 55.5, avg margin 47.1; avg tier-1 subs 1.7/game; avg tier-2/3 subs 6.9/game; tier 1 reached 9/20 (45%), tier 2 20/20 (100%), tier 3 14/20 (70%); Q2 blowout trigger 13/20 (65%).
validation_claims.check_method: Re-run 20 simulations Alabama vs Alabama State with seeds 100-119 and tally tier triggers and scores.

validation_claims.claim: 20-game even matchup test (Alabama vs Auburn, seeds 200-219): blowout subs triggered in 17/20 (85%); the 3 games with zero blowout triggers (seeds 202, 214, 217) were wire-to-wire close contests with final margins under 7 points.
validation_claims.check_method: Re-run 20 simulations Alabama vs Auburn with seeds 200-219 and tally trigger rates by final margin.

non_actions.item: Lower flat threshold (20 instead of 25) — alternative not adopted.
non_actions.reason: Would catch more games but is too aggressive for Q3 20-point leads in a 9-point-TD sport. A 20-point lead is ~2.5 touchdowns — well within comeback range.

non_actions.item: Time-of-possession based trigger — alternative not adopted.
non_actions.reason: Rejected because TOP correlates weakly with game outcome in Viperball's high-scoring format — a team can dominate TOP with field goals while the opponent scores touchdowns on short drives.

non_actions.item: Explicit 'garbage time' mode (formal game state flag changing all simulation parameters at once) — alternative not adopted.
non_actions.reason: Rejected in favor of composing existing systems (substitutions + play-calling + tempo) because it's more maintainable and each component can be tuned independently.

non_actions.item: Coach personality gating (only conservative coaches pull starters early).
non_actions.reason: Rejected for the initial implementation because the behavior should be universal — even aggressive coaches pull starters in 35-point blowouts. Future work could modulate the tier thresholds based on coaching personality.

residual_risks: Viperball scores are inherently high. Even with suppressed play-calling, a team's backups running dives in a 35-point blowout will still score occasionally. The system dampens scoring but doesn't eliminate it. Final scores in mismatches will still look large by football standards.
Tier 1 rotation is probabilistic. The 60% trigger chance and 2-per-drive cap mean tier 1 behavior varies game-to-game.

Clock-chew stacks aggressively. In theory, a run play's weight could be boosted 4-5x through cascading multipliers while kick pass weight is suppressed to near-zero — worth monitoring for games where the lead narrows.
open_hooks: Future work could modulate the tier thresholds based on coaching personality (e.g., aggressive coaches require 5 more points to trigger each tier).
section_headers_original: Architecture Decision Record: Graduated Blowout Backup Rotation
Why graduated tiers instead of a lower flat threshold?
Why couple play-calling changes with substitutions?
20-Game Mismatch Test (Alabama vs Alabama State, seeds 100-119)
20-Game Even Matchup Test (Alabama vs Auburn, seeds 200-219)
unmapped_content: Tier table: Tier 0 Normal (<20 pts, or Q1/early Q2) — none; Tier 1 Soft Rotation (20+ pts Q3+, or 25+ pts late Q2) — rotate 1-2 highest-usage starters out per drive (60% chance each), blowout clock-chew active; Tier 2 Pull Starters (25+ pts Q3+, or 30+ late Q2, or 20+ pts under 5:00 Q4) — all skill-position starters benched for game; Tier 3 Deep Bench (35+ pts Q3+) — skill + defensive starters benched. Q4 rule rationale: 'There is no competitive reason for starters to be on the field with a 20-point lead and less than 5 minutes in the game.' Positive consequences: backups accumulate meaningful stats feeding portal calculations, award consideration, development tracking; final scores come down in mismatches; comeback potential increases ('backups are measurably worse... This creates organic drama without scripting it'); starters stay healthy (less injury exposure compounding over a dynasty season). Even-matchup observation: 'In the 2 close games where blowout subs still fired (seeds 200, 204), one team built a large mid-game lead that later evaporated — the backup rotation itself may have contributed to the comeback, which is a realistic and desirable emergent behavior.' Files Changed: engine/game_engine.py +136/-17. Related Documents: Coaching Substitution System AAR (aar_2026-03-20_coaching_substitutions.md), Fatigue System Overhaul PRD (prd_fatigue_system_overhaul_2026_03_20.md), Lead Management Countermeasures AAR (AAR_lead_management_countermeasures.md). Metadata: Status: Implemented; Branch: claude/backup-rotation-blowout-Bk2IG.
parser_notes: Titled Architecture Decision Record (status Implemented), not an AAR, but follows the full AAR shape (context/why, decision/design, validation with seeds, consequences/risks, alternatives rejected) — genre_confidence medium per assignment. 'Alternatives Considered' mapped into non_actions as rejected options.
